title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who discuss their experiences with public speaking. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Finding Your Voice: Four People on Public Speaking and Communication

texts.D:
### Leon, 33, Secondary School Teacher
[46]Teaching thirty teenagers for six hours a day has made me comfortable in front of a crowd in a way that no formal training ever could.[/46] I think the classroom is the best public speaking school in existence, because the consequences of losing your audience are immediate and visible. [50]I've noticed that the skills I use to hold a class's attention – varying pace, using questions, watching for signs of confusion – translate directly into effective presentations in other settings.[/50] A colleague once asked me to step in and deliver an unplanned keynote at a conference because another speaker dropped out, and I said yes without hesitation. That spontaneity comes from classroom experience, not talent.
texts.A:
### Sandra, 56, HR Manager
[45]Over the years I have trained hundreds of employees in communication skills, so people sometimes assume I am a naturally confident speaker.[/45] In fact, I was trained, not born – I worked very hard for many years to develop the skills I now have. [49]The most common mistake I see in new presenters is focusing on what they're going to say rather than on who they're speaking to, which usually results in a technically competent but emotionally disconnected performance.[/49] [52]I always tell trainees that good communication is fundamentally about curiosity – you have to be genuinely interested in your audience's reaction to have any hope of engaging them.[/52]
texts.B:
### Tim, 27, Postgraduate Student
[44]I've always found one-to-one conversation easy, but being asked to speak in front of a group makes my mind go completely blank.[/44] I joined a debating society at university specifically to confront this fear, even though every session for the first semester felt like an ordeal. [48]The turning point came when I gave a three-minute speech on a subject I genuinely cared about and felt, for the first time, that the audience was actually listening rather than judging.[/48] I still feel nervous beforehand, but the nerves feel manageable now rather than overwhelming. My thesis defence is coming up, and I no longer dread it the way I once would have.
texts.C:
### Rebecca, 41, Sales Director
[43]I was terrified of public speaking until I was in my early thirties, despite having a job that required me to present to clients every week.[/43] I had developed a range of coping strategies that hid my anxiety from others, but I was exhausted by the performance. A colleague recommended an improvisational theatre class, which seemed absurd to me at the time. [47]Improv taught me that the fear of public speaking mostly comes from trying to be perfect, and that audiences respond far better to someone who is genuine than someone who is flawless.[/47] [51]I now actively seek out opportunities to present in front of large audiences because I find it genuinely energising rather than draining.[/51]

questions.question: concealed a significant fear of public speaking from those around them for many years?
questions.explanation: Rebecca was terrified of public speaking but had developed coping strategies that hid her anxiety from others, which was exhausting.

questions.question: finds informal communication straightforward but struggles specifically when speaking to a group?
questions.explanation: Tim says one-to-one conversation is easy but being asked to speak in front of a group makes his mind go blank.

questions.question: is often mistakenly believed to have a natural gift for public speaking?
questions.explanation: People assume Sandra is a naturally confident speaker because she trains others, but she says she was trained, not born – she worked very hard to develop her skills.

questions.question: attributes their confidence in public speaking primarily to their professional daily experience?
questions.explanation: Leon says teaching teenagers for six hours a day has made him comfortable in front of a crowd in a way no formal training could.

questions.question: learned that authenticity is more effective than technical perfection when addressing an audience?
questions.explanation: Improv taught Rebecca that fear of public speaking mostly comes from trying to be perfect, and that audiences prefer someone genuine over someone flawless.

questions.question: describes a particular moment when they first felt a genuine connection with their audience?
questions.explanation: Tim's turning point was giving a speech on a subject he cared about and feeling for the first time that the audience was listening rather than judging.

questions.question: identifies a widespread error that undermines many speakers' ability to connect with listeners?
questions.explanation: Sandra says the most common mistake new presenters make is focusing on what they're saying rather than who they're speaking to, resulting in a disconnected performance.

questions.question: notes that techniques developed in one context can be effectively applied to other speaking situations?
questions.explanation: Leon observes that the classroom skills he uses to hold attention – varying pace, asking questions, watching for confusion – translate directly into effective presentations elsewhere.

questions.question: describes a shift from dreading public speaking to actively enjoying it?
questions.explanation: Rebecca now actively seeks opportunities to present in front of large audiences because she finds it genuinely energising rather than draining.

questions.question: offers a fundamental principle they believe underpins all effective communication?
questions.explanation: Sandra tells trainees that good communication is fundamentally about curiosity – being genuinely interested in the audience's reaction.
